_note: Ernest particle_sort batch 1 — 15 items at B1-B2, mid-frequency PVs in school / sports / gaming / brit_expat themes. Single-particle blank; 1 correct + 3 distractors. Difficulty 2-3. Authored to library; surfaces in Coach tab when PV enters Ernest's active window. Coach explanations in English per family-profiles 2026-05-01 decision. Closes the recognition-side of the input-gap-by-30pts pattern noted in Ernest's profile.

exercises.sentence: I just found {1} that the trip got cancelled because of the storm.
exercises.meaning: discover or learn something previously unknown
exercises.reasoning: 'Find out' = discover / learn (a fact). 'Find up' is not standard. 'Find off' is not standard. 'Find in' is not standard. The 'out' = something previously unknown is now known to you. Common with bad news.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For discovering a fact: 'find out'. Russian 'узнать' → 'find out'.

exercises.sentence: If you don't know a word, you can always look it {1} in the dictionary.
exercises.meaning: find information in a reference source
exercises.reasoning: 'Look up' a word / fact = find it in a dictionary or online. 'Look over' = scan briefly (a document). 'Look for' = search (a lost thing). 'Look into' = investigate (a topic, a complaint). Reference-lookup is specifically 'look up'.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For finding a word in a dictionary: 'look up'. Russian 'посмотреть в словаре' → 'look up'.

exercises.sentence: The school sent home a form for parents to fill {1} before the trip.
exercises.meaning: complete a form by writing in the spaces
exercises.reasoning: 'Fill in' a form (BrE) = complete the spaces. 'Fill out' is the American equivalent — also correct. 'Fill up' = make full (a fuel tank). 'Fill on' is not standard. UK forms tend to use 'fill in'.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For completing a form: 'fill in' (UK) or 'fill out' (US). 'Fill up' is for fuel/glasses.

exercises.sentence: The science teacher asked us to carry {1} a small experiment at home and write up the results.
exercises.meaning: perform or conduct an action (an experiment, a task)
exercises.reasoning: 'Carry out' = perform (an experiment, an investigation, an order). Formal register for executing a task. 'Carry over' = transfer to a different period. 'Carry on' = continue. 'Carry off' = succeed at something difficult / win a prize.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For performing a task: 'carry out'. Russian 'провести (эксперимент)' → 'carry out'.

exercises.sentence: I haven't seen Tom in ages — we should catch {1} this weekend.
exercises.meaning: meet someone after time apart to share news
exercises.reasoning: 'Catch up' (with someone) = meet / talk after time apart. Also: 'catch up on' work = do what you missed. 'Catch on' = become popular / understand. 'Catch in' is not standard. 'Catch out' = expose someone in an error.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For meeting a friend after time apart: 'catch up'. Russian 'встретиться, поболтать' → 'catch up'.

exercises.sentence: We just hung {1} at the food court for a couple of hours after class.
exercises.reasoning: 'Hang out' = spend casual time (with friends, at a place). 'Hang up' = end a phone call / hang on a hook. 'Hang around' is similar but more aimless / waiting. 'Hang on' = wait / hold. 'Hang out' is the planned-social-time sense.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For spending casual time with friends: 'hang out'. Russian 'тусоваться' / 'провести время' → 'hang out'.

exercises.sentence: Only half the team showed {1} for practice on Saturday — coach was not happy.
exercises.meaning: arrive at a place or event (often informally)
exercises.reasoning: 'Show up' = arrive (often unannounced or simply turn up). 'Show off' = display proudly / boast. 'Show in' = lead someone inside (formal). 'Show out' = lead someone out / a behaviour pattern. 'Show up' is the informal attendance verb.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For arriving at an event: 'show up'. Russian 'появиться / прийти' → 'show up'.

exercises.sentence: After three failed attempts at the boss fight, I almost gave {1} for the night.
exercises.reasoning: 'Give up' = stop trying (voluntarily). 'Give in' = surrender / yield to pressure. 'Give out' = stop functioning (legs gave out) / distribute. 'Give away' = donate / reveal a secret. Subtle: 'give up' is your own decision; 'give in' is responding to pressure.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For stopping trying: 'give up'. Russian 'сдаться / бросить' → 'give up'.

exercises.sentence: I try to work {1} at the gym at least three times a week.
exercises.reasoning: 'Work out' = exercise (gym, sports). Also: solve (a problem) / end up well. 'Work off' = lose by exertion ('work off the weekend pizza'). 'Work on' = improve through practice (a skill). 'Work up' = build up gradually (a sweat, an appetite).
exercises.fallback_feedback: For physical exercise: 'work out'. Russian 'тренироваться' → 'work out'.

exercises.sentence: The school bus broke {1} halfway home — we waited an hour for a replacement.
exercises.meaning: stop functioning (of a vehicle or machine)
exercises.reasoning: 'Break down' (machine) = stop working. Also: cry uncontrollably / fail (of negotiations). 'Break up' = end a relationship / disintegrate. 'Break off' = end abruptly (a relationship, a conversation). 'Break through' = make a major advance.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For a vehicle failing: 'break down'. Russian 'сломаться (о машине)' → 'break down'.

exercises.sentence: Our group needs to come {1} with three project ideas by Friday.
exercises.meaning: produce or invent (an idea, a plan, an answer)
exercises.reasoning: 'Come up with' = produce / invent (an idea, a solution, a name). Always followed by 'with' before the noun. 'Come out' = appear / be released. 'Come across' = encounter / find by chance. 'Come over' = visit. The 'up + with' combination is the idea-producing PV.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For producing an idea: 'come up with'. Russian 'придумать' → 'come up with'.

exercises.sentence: Can we set {1} a quick gaming session for tonight? Around eight?
exercises.reasoning: 'Set up' = arrange (a meeting, a game, an account). 'Set off' = begin a journey / trigger (an alarm). 'Set out' = depart / present in writing. 'Set in' = start and persist (winter has set in). 'Set up' is the schedule / configure sense.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For arranging a game session: 'set up'. Russian 'устроить / организовать' → 'set up'.

exercises.sentence: Several students from our year are taking part {1} the maths olympiad next month.
exercises.meaning: participate in an event or activity
exercises.reasoning: 'Take part in' = participate in (an event, a competition). The 'in' is obligatory before the event. 'Take part at' / 'on' / 'of' are not standard. Russian 'участвовать в' has the same pattern — straight transfer.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For participating in a competition: 'take part in'. Russian 'участвовать в' → 'take part in'.

exercises.sentence: I get along really well {1} my history teacher — she explains everything clearly.
exercises.meaning: have a good relationship with someone
exercises.reasoning: 'Get along with' someone = have a good relationship. The 'with' is obligatory before the person. 'Get on with' is the UK equivalent — also widely used. 'Get along to / by / from' are not standard for relationships.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For having a good relationship: 'get along with' (or 'get on with' in UK). Russian 'ладить с' → 'get along with'.

exercises.sentence: The football tournament has been put {1} until next term because of the weather.
exercises.reasoning: 'Put off' = postpone (a meeting, an event, a decision). Also: discourage or repel ('the smell put me off'). 'Put on' = wear clothing / stage a show. 'Put out' = extinguish (a fire) / inconvenience someone. 'Put away' = store / imprison.
exercises.fallback_feedback: For postponing an event: 'put off'. Russian 'отложить / перенести' → 'put off'.
